findings.title: The stimulus generator was selected for penalty magnitude, and the claim-grade cross-model numbers rest on that Claude-shaped distribution
findings.target: docs/preregistration_tierB.md §Design ('haiku matched/beat opus on validator yield (76% vs 60%), behavioral screen-in (55% vs 48%), and penalty magnitude on screened-in pairs') vs technical/#models ('Every band sits below zero', the 'claim-grade table') and methods.html#limitations bullet 6
findings.argument: The Tier B generator was chosen in part because its pairs produced larger penalties — the study selected its test-writer on the dependent variable. Tier B now dominates every displayed estimate (gemma-2-2b: ~680 phrases vs 132 Tier A), so the headline 'every band sits below zero' quantifies the penalty on a stimulus distribution optimized to show it. Cross-model replication does not cure this: all eight models read the same Claude-authored one-term-swap, single-token-target frames. The prereg's own guard — 'generator (haiku vs opus/sonnet Tier A) enters as a fixed effect in pooled models' — is nowhere executed in the published data/model_stats.json (no generator covariate or Tier A/B split), yet the numbers carry the 'claim-grade' label. The limitations page discloses that stimuli are constructed but never that the constructor was chosen for effect size.
findings.fix: Publish the pre-registered generator-stratified analysis (Tier A vs Tier B mean penalty per model) next to the forest plot; add one sentence to methods.html#generation-eval and the limitations disclosing the penalty-magnitude criterion in generator selection; report the hand-built (non-LLM-authored) set's penalty as a benchmark row in the claim-grade table.

findings.title: Translation 'restores it' is an uncontrolled Claude round-trip evaluated on a downgrade-selected set
findings.target: index.html lede ('rewriting the sentence in clinical terms restores it'), translation/index.html ('Translating the patient sentence back into clinical terms restores the prediction'), methods.html#generation-eval ('strict separation of church and state')
findings.argument: Three compounding problems. (1) Circularity: the patient sentences are Claude paraphrases of clinical content, and the mitigation asks a Claude model to invert them — recovery measures how well one model family round-trips its own paraphrases; no published result tests translation on the hand-built real-patient-language set, and the auditor of the translation step is also a Claude model, so the 'church and state' framing overstates the separation. (2) Selection: per the prereg, the translated panel fires only on flagged downgrades — pairs chosen for extreme patient-side failure — so regression to the mean guarantees apparent recovery on some cases under any rewrite. (3) No placebo: the site's own paraphrase_robustness record (data/provenance.json) shows innocuous rewording moves these probabilities by mean |Δ| 0.064 — the same order as the recoveries celebrated — and the placebo-paraphrase translation arm is still listed as 'queued' on technical/, while the steering claim (correctly) has its 0/5 placebo. The front-page present-tense 'restores it' is not yet earned.
findings.fix: Run and publish the queued placebo-paraphrase arm before keeping 'restores it' in the lede (interim: 'restored the clinical continuation in 8 of 20 selected downgrade cases; placebo control pending'); add a translation panel on the hand-built set; name the translator and auditor as the same model family as the generator in the church-and-state paragraph.

findings.title: A published 45%→41% re-trace discrepancy contradicts the 'zero repeat variation' repeatability claim
findings.target: methods.html step 2 ('every repeat reproduced identical probabilities… Zero repeat variation has been observed so far'; data/retrace_consistency.json prob_spread_max 0.0) vs translation/index.html ('45% in the July 7 study trace, 41% on the July 9 re-trace') and data/provenance.json retrace_20260709 ('the difference is re-trace jitter, quantified in paraphrase_robustness')
findings.argument: Two public artifacts assert mutually exclusive facts about the same instrument. If July 9 re-traced the same translated prompt, a 4.4-point probability difference falsifies 'zero repeat variation', and the 0-of-60/68 record is silently scoped to whatever retrace_consistency.py compares (evidently excluding translated panels). If instead the translator was re-run and emitted a different sentence, then the translation page's 'independent re-trace of the study case' and provenance.json's 're-trace jitter' are mislabels — it is a paraphrase comparison, and pointing at paraphrase_robustness to 'quantify' re-trace jitter concedes exactly that. A referee cannot determine which from the published record. Separately, the designed 60-trace sample was three same-day CI runs (docs/repeatability_sample.md), so it evidences within-day determinism, not stability over time — the doc scopes this correctly, but methods.html generalizes to 'the instrument repeats itself'.
findings.fix: State on both pages precisely what differed between July 7 and July 9 (prompt text, graph settings, translator re-run) and relabel accordingly; scope the methods claim to the panels and quantities retrace_consistency.py actually compares, explicitly noting translated panels are outside the 0-differences record if they are.

findings.title: Post-registration model additions presented at claim grade; a marginal exploratory result (medgemma) promoted into the headline narrative
findings.target: technical/#models 'claim-grade table' + endnote ('Part 4's cross-model statistics are the claim-grade interim numbers, with a pre-registered holdout still sealed') vs docs/prereg_divergence_log.md row 3 ('extra models are secondary/exploratory; primary endpoints stay on the pre-registered four'); methods.html#limitations ('The one medically tuned model measured, medgemma-4b-it… still shows a below-zero penalty')
findings.argument: The prereg fixed four primary models; the site's table mixes them indistinguishably with four post-hoc additions (gemma-2-2b-it, llama-3.2-3b, medgemma-4b-it, olmo-2-1b) under wording that borrows pre-registration credibility for the whole set. The divergence log's own disposition ('secondary/exploratory') appears nowhere on the site. Worst case: medgemma-4b-it — probed 2026-07-13, n=119 interim, penalty CI [−6.9, −0.2] pp with the upper bound 0.22 pp from zero, downgrade asymmetry non-significant after correction (q=0.075) — carries the rhetorically strongest sentence on the methods page (medical tuning doesn't remove the penalty). That claim could flip with a shift of a quarter of its CI width and was never pre-registered.
findings.fix: Tag the four added models 'exploratory · added post-registration' in the table and link the divergence log; reword the medgemma limitation to quote its interim CI and exploratory status, or hold it until the sealed-holdout endpoint analysis.

findings.title: Front-door per-pair numbers violate the prereg's own paraphrase-noise rule, and the flagship is an undisclosed extreme-tail pick
findings.target: docs/preregistration_tierB.md ('no per-pair claim is reported without its paraphrase-noise caveat') vs index.html scenario-85 demo and og:image:alt (inhaler 69% → 4%), wording-differences/ ('Language Penalty: −65%'), phrase-dataset/ ('plumber — at 68%'), translation/ grandma case
findings.argument: The site's own robustness measurement (provenance.json paraphrase_robustness: innocuous rewording moves single-pair probabilities by mean |Δ| 0.064, median per-baseline SD 0.042; verdict 'single pairs are illustrations, inference lives in the paired aggregate') appears on zero pages — I checked; the only 'paraphrase' mention site-wide is a queued-work bullet. Every flagship per-pair number is presented uncaveated, breaching a rule the prereg fixed in advance. Compounding this, scenario 85's −65 pp swing is ~19× the population mean penalty (−3.5 pp, CI [−4.6, −2.5]) and was surfaced by the exporter's consequence ranking (flips first, then |penalty|, 25-render cap); the scenarios page discloses the ranking, but the front page presents the tail case as 'the live case' with no base-rate anchor adjacent to it.
findings.fix: Surface paraphrase_robustness into a fetched data file and render a one-line caveat beside each per-pair headline ('single-pair probabilities move ±6 pp under innocuous rewording; claims are aggregate'); caption scenario 85 on index.html as 'the largest measured effect of N phrases (mean penalty −3.5 pp)'.

findings.title: Target screening conditions on the clinical side of the differenced quantity with no published sensitivity check
findings.target: prereg 'Screening: --screen-targets 0.02'; simulated-scenarios/ and index.html counts ('measured · screened out'); flip rates computed over screened-in pairs only
findings.argument: Screening keeps a pair only when the clinical-side target clears the 0.02 floor (patient trace then skipped otherwise), so retained pairs are conditioned on the clinical side of penalty = p_patient − p_clinical being high enough to measure. With per-pair noise of the order the study itself measured (~4–8 pp), this conditioning biases retained penalties negative near the floor via regression to the mean, and it is asymmetric — patient-side unmeasurability never screens a pair out. All displayed flip rates and penalties are computed on this conditioned subset, and no artifact reports how the estimates move with the threshold, which the free logits backend could test at zero cost.
findings.fix: Publish a one-off threshold sensitivity analysis (penalty and flip rate at screens 0 / 0.01 / 0.02 / 0.05 on the CPU-logits backend) and add a sentence to simulated-scenarios/ stating the direction of the conditioning.

findings.title: 'The study' has no version anchor: unstamped mutable data files, stale hard-coded numbers, and an out-of-prereg batch feeding the displayed series
findings.target: data/model_stats.json and data/urgency_shift.json (no generated_utc/commit field); footer claim 'every number on this page traces to a data file in this repository'; methods.html static '52 pairs re-traced' (data now 68); retrace_consistency.json note 'not a designed sample' (20 of 68 pairs are the designed sample per docs/repeatability_sample.md); index.html Fig 4 '0.45 … July 7 trace' vs translation/ '41% … July 9 re-trace'; convergence.json final points labeled through pairs_20260713T135755Z, which provenance.json marks 'kept outside the pre-registered run' (emergency set)
findings.argument: With batches landing nightly, the claim-grade table's values change under the same URL with no as-of stamp, making any citation of the site unreproducible a day later; the drift is already visible in stale fallback text (52 vs 68), a repeat-record note that became false when the designed sample was pooled in, two pages headlining different recovery numbers for the same flagship case, and adjacent figures on technical/ giving gemma-2-2b three different n's (forest 680, convergence final 650, models_meta n_traced 527) with no reconciliation. Most concretely, the convergence series consumed the emergency batch that the provenance file itself says is kept outside the pre-registered run.
findings.fix: Emit generated_utc plus engine commit into model_stats.json and urgency_shift.json and render 'as of <date> · <sha>' under the forest plot and claim-grade table; correct the retrace_consistency note to 'opportunistic + designed (20 pairs × 3 runs)'; reconcile or footnote the differing n's; exclude the emergency-set batch from prereg-facing series (or label its points) and align the two Fig-4 recovery headlines.
